content:
Deep learning grew from decades of **neural network research** constrained by data and compute.

**Perceptrons** (Rosenblatt, 1950s) and **multilayer networks** showed nonlinear learning was possible, but training deep stacks was difficult.

**Backpropagation** (popularized in the 1980s) made gradient-based training of multi-layer nets practical.

The **AI winter** periods cooled hype when compute and datasets could not support reliable large networks.

**LeNet** and convolutional nets (1990s) demonstrated representation learning for digits and document recognition.

The **ImageNet era** (2012 onward) — AlexNet, GPUs, and massive labeled image sets — proved deep CNNs could leap past hand-crafted pipelines on vision.

**Sequence models, attention, and transformers** extended representation learning to language and multimodal data.

Today, **large-scale deep networks** underpin speech, vision, ranking, and the generative models discussed in Generative AI — while classical ML remains vital outside unstructured, data-rich regimes.

timeline.label: 1950s–1960s
timeline.description: Perceptron and early neural nets establish layered computation but face limits on depth and hardware.

timeline.label: 1980s
timeline.description: Backpropagation revives training of multi-layer networks; representation learning becomes practically optimizable.
LeNet-5 shows convolutional deep nets excel on digit and document recognition.
AlexNet on ImageNet marks the modern deep learning surge — GPUs plus data plus depth.

timeline.label: 2017–present
timeline.description: Transformers and scale drive language and generative breakthroughs built on deep representation learning.
